text: Single site robotic assisted tubal anastomosis. In this video, we will review a case of tubal ligation reversal, describe our technique for tubal anastomosis, and demonstrate the feasibility of single site robotic assisted tubal anastomosis. Tubal ligation is the most common form of contraception used by couples today. However, up to twenty percent of women will experience regret and up to five percent of women who undergo tubal ligation will request a reversal.

Minimally invasive surgery has come to replace laparotomy as the method of choice through which to achieve tubal anastomosis. Microsurgical laparoscopic tubal anastomosis is technically challenging and requires significant expertise to achieve adequate outcomes. Robotic surgery may then provide multiple advantages over laparoscopy, particularly for tubal anastomosis, given its three-D view, magnified micro-articulated instruments, ergonomic natural hand movements, and suppression of physical tremor. Single-site robotic surgery may further offer improved healing, given the presence of only one surgical incision, which may be clinically relevant in the patients at the highest risk of surgical complications or poor wound healing.

Rates of successful tubal permeability with robotic surgery, however, still vary between twenty to eighty-nine percent, and so standardization of surgical technique is still lacking. Here we present a case of a thirty-three-year-old morbidly obese woman with a history of bilateral tubal ligation in two thousand seven. She since experienced regret and desired a second child.

A 2.5 cm fascial incision is made in the umbilicus to access the abdominal cavity, where the da Vinci single site robotic platform is introduced. The surgeon conducts an abdominal survey, identifying 2 cm proximal and 4-5 cm distal fallopian tubes bilaterally. Using monopolar electrosurgery at 20 watts of continuous power, areas of adhesions are resected, and occlusive devices such as fallope rings and filchi clips are mobilized from the fallopian tubes.

Dilute vasopressin is injected into the mesosalpinx, revealing significant anterior wall adiposity. The surgeon uses monopolar electrosurgery to free the fallopian tube segments from the underlying mesosalpinx, facilitating a tension-free suture line. Scissors are used to cut both ends of the adjoining fallopian tube, ensuring patency while preserving tissue length. Approximately five centimeters are removed from the obstructed ends to achieve patency. Chromoperturbation reveals blue dye, confirming patency in both proximal and distal segments. The lumens are identified, and tissue quality is confirmed with pink, well-perfused tissue at the ends of each fallopian tube segment.

The surgeon re-approximates the defect in the mesosalpinx with two stay sutures of 6-0 PDS at the junction between the mesosalpinx and the six o'clock portion of both fallopian tube edges. Tubal reanastomosis is performed using an 8-0 Surgipro suture, employing an outside-to-inside and inside-to-outside technique at cardinal headings to avoid knots in the lumen. A 6-0 Prolene suture reinforces the suture line along the fallopian tube serosa to prevent bowel adhesions. The procedure is repeated on the contralateral side, confirmed by chromopertubation showing fill and spill bilaterally.

Intraoperative limitations of robotic assisted tubal anastomosis include a lack of haptic feedback, leading to an eleven percent rate of suture rupture quoted in the literature. There is also a longer operative time associated with robotic versus laparoscopic tubal anastomosis. However, the shortened learning curve with robotic surgery allows for a quicker rate of proficiency. Perioperative considerations should include the increased fixed costs associated with robotic surgery. Therefore, use of this platform should be individualized to patient and physician needs.

In summary, here we have demonstrated the feasibility of single-site robotic-assisted tubal anastomosis, allowing for the microsurgical precision of robotic surgery and the perioperative benefits of a minimally invasive approach.
